title: STEADI Falls Screening: The CDC Framework Explained
description: STEADI (Stopping Elderly Accidental Death and Injury) explained. The CDC's three-step falls prevention framework: screen, assess, intervene.
featuredImageAlt: Care worker and older adult during falls screening session
author: Able Care Clinical Team
excerpt: STEADI is the CDC's clinical framework for systematic fall prevention. Learn how screen, assess, intervene works and how Able Assess implements it digitally.

blocks.heading: STEADI provides the framework for evidence-based fall prevention.
blocks.bodyText: See how Able Assess implements the CDC's three-step process in a single digital workflow.
blocks.primaryCtaText: See How Able Assess Aligns with STEADI

body:
## What is STEADI?

STEADI stands for Stopping Elderly Accidental Death and Injury. It is the CDC's evidence-based clinical framework for systematic fall prevention in older adults. Developed by the CDC's Injury Prevention and Control division, STEADI provides a structured three-step process that healthcare providers can integrate into routine clinical practice.

The framework was designed to address a critical gap. Despite strong evidence that falls are preventable, most healthcare settings lacked a systematic approach to identifying and managing fall risk. Screening was inconsistent, assessment varied by provider and intervention was often reactive rather than proactive.

STEADI changes this by providing a standardized pathway: screen every older adult, assess those at elevated risk and intervene with targeted strategies. The framework is endorsed by the American Geriatrics Society, the National Council on Aging and multiple state health departments.

Since its introduction, STEADI has been adopted across primary care, emergency departments, hospital systems and community health programs. Research demonstrates that systematic STEADI implementation increases identification of at-risk individuals by 50% to 70% compared to opportunistic assessment. The framework aligns with NICE 2025 falls guidance in the United Kingdom and the 2022 World Guidelines for Falls Prevention.

## The three-step process

### Step 1: Screen

The first step is universal screening. STEADI recommends that every adult aged 65 and older is asked three core questions annually. Have you fallen in the past year? Do you feel unsteady when standing or walking? Do you worry about falling?

A positive answer to any of these questions triggers progression to step two. The screening takes one to two minutes and can be administered by any member of the care team, including non-clinical staff. It can be incorporated into routine intake procedures, annual wellness visits or community health events.

The simplicity of the screening step is deliberate. The barrier to systematic fall prevention has never been the complexity of the assessment. It has been the failure to ask the questions in the first place. STEADI ensures that every older adult is screened, every time.

### Step 2: Assess

For individuals who screen positive, STEADI prescribes a comprehensive risk factor assessment. This typically takes 15 to 30 minutes and includes multiple components.

Functional assessment involves objective physical measurements. The Timed Up and Go test measures functional mobility and dynamic balance. The 30-second chair stand test assesses lower limb strength. Gait speed measurement evaluates walking ability. Grip strength testing provides a proxy for whole-body strength and physiological reserve.

Medical assessment covers medication review, focusing on sedatives, antihypertensives and psychotropics. Visual acuity is checked. Postural hypotension is evaluated. Foot health and footwear are assessed. Cognitive status is considered where relevant.

Environmental assessment evaluates the home or care setting for trip hazards, lighting adequacy, handrail availability and bathroom safety. This component is most relevant for community-dwelling individuals.

The assessment step identifies which specific risk factors are present for each individual. This is critical because fall prevention is most effective when interventions are targeted to actual risk factors rather than applied generically.

### Step 3: Intervene

STEADI interventions are matched to the risk factors identified during assessment. This targeted approach is supported by Cochrane evidence showing that multifactorial interventions reduce fall rates by approximately 24%.

For individuals with strength and balance deficits, STEADI recommends referral to evidence-based exercise programs. Programs should include balance challenge, progressive strength training and adequate dose of at least three hours per week.

For medication-related risk, structured pharmacist-led review can reduce or substitute high-risk medications. For vision impairment, referral for eye examination and correction. For environmental hazards, home modification by an occupational therapist.

The intervention step also includes patient education, care coordination and follow-up screening to measure whether interventions are effective. STEADI recommends re-screening at defined intervals to track changes in risk status.

## Implementing STEADI in care settings

### Primary care

Primary care is the most common setting for STEADI implementation. The screening questions integrate into annual wellness visits. Positive screens trigger in-clinic functional assessment or referral to a falls prevention service. Primary care is well positioned for medication review and coordination of specialist referrals.

### Hospitals and emergency departments

Emergency departments see a high volume of older adults who have already fallen. STEADI screening in the ED identifies patients at risk of recurrent falls and triggers assessment and intervention before discharge. The SAFE Act is making this screening mandatory in Medicare-funded facilities.

### Aged care and residential settings

Residential care settings can implement STEADI as part of routine admission assessment and ongoing monitoring. The framework provides a structured approach to falls prevention that satisfies regulatory requirements from CQC, Joint Commission and state survey agencies.

### Implementation steps

Successful STEADI implementation requires several organizational steps.

- Designate a falls prevention champion to lead the program.
- Train staff on the screening questions and assessment protocols.
- Establish referral pathways for each intervention category.
- Implement a data system to track screening rates, risk classifications and outcomes.
- Set targets for screening coverage and monitor compliance.
- Review program data quarterly to identify gaps and improve processes.

## Able Assess and STEADI alignment

Able Assess implements the STEADI framework digitally. The platform guides assessors through each step of the process, from initial screening questions through comprehensive functional assessment to intervention recommendations.

The screening questions are presented exactly as validated by the CDC. Functional assessments, including Timed Up and Go, sit-to-stand, gait speed and grip strength, are captured using the GripAble sensor with automatic scoring against normative data. Risk classification follows STEADI criteria.

For organizations implementing STEADI, Able Assess solves the consistency problem. Every assessor follows the same protocol. Scoring is automatic. Data capture is complete. The platform ensures that STEADI is implemented as designed, not as interpreted by individual staff members.

The result is a defensible, auditable record of systematic fall prevention that aligns with CDC guidance, NICE 2025 recommendations and the requirements of the SAFE Act.

## Frequently asked questions

### Is STEADI mandatory?

STEADI itself is not mandatory. It is a recommended framework published by the CDC. However, the SAFE Act, currently progressing through Congress, mandates systematic fall risk screening in Medicare-funded emergency departments and hospitals. STEADI is the most widely recognized framework for meeting this requirement.

### Who can administer STEADI screening?

The initial screening questions can be administered by any member of the care team, including non-clinical staff. The comprehensive assessment step typically involves clinical input, though digital platforms like Able Assess enable non-clinical staff to conduct objective functional assessments with guided workflows.

### How often should STEADI screening be repeated?

The CDC recommends annual screening for all adults aged 65 and older. For individuals identified as at risk, more frequent reassessment is appropriate, typically every three to six months, to track changes in risk status and measure intervention effectiveness.

### Does STEADI work in non-US settings?

Yes. The STEADI framework is based on international evidence and aligns with falls prevention guidelines used globally. In the United Kingdom, STEADI aligns closely with NICE 2025 falls guidance. The screening questions, functional assessments and intervention principles are applicable across healthcare systems.

### What is the difference between STEADI and other falls screening tools?

STEADI is a framework, not a single tool. It provides the overarching structure of screen, assess, intervene. Within that framework, specific validated tools are used at each step. The Timed Up and Go, 30-second chair stand, gait speed and grip strength are tools used within the STEADI assessment step. Able Assess implements the full STEADI framework using these validated tools in a single digital workflow.
